elements.sent-2411-650: The term Duplex originates from the field of communications, used to describe interaction modes between two parties in data transmission.
elements.sent-2411-651: Depending on the type of communication, duplex is divided into half-duplex and full-duplex.
elements.sent-2411-652: With the development of audio processing and generation technology , the concept of duplex has been introduced to speech systems, especially within the context of speech language models.
elements.sent-2411-653: Here, duplex doesn’t just refer to signal transmission but emphasizes the synchronization and natural interaction in human-computer dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-654: Specifically, within model architecture, it means that the model must retain its ability to perceive external input even while generating a response---essentially, the ability to listen while speaking.. Simplex.
elements.sent-2411-655: In simplex communication, data flows in only one direction.
elements.sent-2411-656: The speaker can send data, while the listener can only receive it.
elements.sent-2411-657: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:simplex , the robot continuously transmits audio, while the user has no ability to respond.}
elements.sent-2411-658: This fixed-direction, one-way communication has the limitation of lacking interactivity.. Half-Duplex.
elements.sent-2411-659: In half-duplex communication, data flows in both directions but not simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-660: The two parties must take turns speaking and listening.
elements.sent-2411-661: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:half-duplex , the user speaks first, followed by a response delay during which the robot "thinks" before replying.}
elements.sent-2411-662: The robot’s response occurs only after the user has finished speaking, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-663: This turn-taking method is similar to using a walkie-talkie, where each party can only transmit after the other has finished, limiting efficiency.Half-duplex is a common mode in early voice interaction systems.
elements.sent-2411-664: In a typical half-duplex interaction, there are noticeable pauses in the conversation; the user and the system cannot “speak” simultaneously, making the conversation feel less smooth, much like communication through a walkie-talkie.
elements.sent-2411-665: For example, voice assistants like Siri use wake words or button presses to trigger the dialogue and require the speaker to finish a complete sentence before responding.
elements.sent-2411-666: These systems typically adopt an ASR-LM-TTS cascaded structure and are often constrained by cascade delays and the turn-based nature of text language models.
elements.sent-2411-667: Although this interaction method is simple and easy to implement, it can feel rigid and disjointed in natural conversational settings, with notable latency.
elements.sent-2411-668: It is designed more for command execution rather than interactive communication.. Full-Duplex.
elements.sent-2411-669: Full-duplex communication allows both parties to send and receive data simultaneously~\cite{ma2024language .}
elements.sent-2411-670: Figure \ref{fig:full-duplex shows the user and robot engaging in overlapping, real-time interaction, where backchannels and interruptions are possible.}
elements.sent-2411-671: This mode enables a natural, two-way conversation, where both the user and robot can speak, respond, and even interrupt each other as needed, much like a phone call.In dialogue systems, full-duplex means that the system and user can speak simultaneously and interrupt each other, making it closer to natural conversation in real life.
elements.sent-2411-672: Full-duplex large voice models allow the system not only to listen and understand the user while they speak but also to interrupt at appropriate moments or respond with backchannel cues.
elements.sent-2411-673: Moreover, the system can detect the user’s intent to interrupt and pause itself accordingly, maintaining a smooth flow in the interaction.

elements.sent-2411-674: The ultimate goal of a spoken dialogue moded is to make the user feel as though they are conversing with a real human friend.

elements.sent-2411-681: We start with some concept.Turn-taking is the core concept in duplex dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-682: It refers to the process in which speakers take turns speaking in an orderly manner during a conversation, forming a pattern of turn-taking.
elements.sent-2411-683: Over the past few decades and has been extensively studied across fields such as linguistics, phonetics, and sociology.

elements.sent-2411-685: It outlines all possible states of turn-taking within an SDS, defining the objective of turn-taking as minimizing mutual silence or overlap between interlocutors, thereby improving communication efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-686: Turn-taking encompasses three fundamental concepts: \quad$\bullet$ Turn-taking cues~\cite{duncan1972some,duncan1974signalling .}
elements.sent-2411-687: These include voice, rhythm, breathing, gaze, or gestures.

elements.sent-2411-691: When the user and agent’s voices overlap, if the user intends to take the turn from the agent, this behavior is defined as an interruption~\cite{khouzaimi2016reinforcement,marge2022spoken .}
elements.sent-2411-692: If the user has no intention of taking the turn, this behavior is considered backchannel~\cite{hara2018prediction or a listener response, such as "uh-huh," "right." Through these concepts, we can better understand turn-taking behavior in duplex dialogues.}
elements.sent-2411-693: In summary, our interactions with voice dialogue systems can be categorized as interruptions, backchannels, and normal turn exchanges.
elements.sent-2411-694: The earliest full-duplex systems used a simple Voice Activity Detection (VAD) component to model whether the user intended to interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-695: However, this approach is inadequate for handling backchannel interaction forms, leading to frequent interruptions and introducing considerable delays.
elements.sent-2411-696: We can briefly categorize the exploration of interactions into cascaded systems and end-to-end systems based on duplex technology.
elements.sent-2411-697: Regardless of the system type, the critical core idea is that the system must continuously track external information in real-time, analyze it, and determine the model’s operational state accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-698: An interactive voice system must meet two requirements: 1) The ability to accept external information in real-time at any moment.

elements.sent-2411-704: In situations where the system detects critical information, it can choose to interrupt the user to provide immediate feedback.
elements.sent-2411-705: For example, if the user is speaking but the system needs to alert them to an error, it can intervene in real-time to ensure effective communication.. Cascaded Systems.

elements.sent-2411-709: In \cite{lin2022duplex , three core modules are proposed to achieve smooth full-duplex dialogue: user state detection, response signal selection, and interruption detection.}
elements.sent-2411-710: The user state detection module not only focuses on traditional turn-end detection but also identifies whether the user intends to switch turns, continue speaking, or hesitates during their speech.
elements.sent-2411-711: To achieve this, the system uses a multimodal model, taking audio and text as inputs, and incorporates features such as speech rhythm, pitch, and pauses for more accurate assessment of the user’s state, determining whether to respond immediately or wait longer.
elements.sent-2411-712: The response signal selection module inserts small backchannel cues (such as "uh-huh" or "right") at appropriate times to simulate natural human conversation.
elements.sent-2411-713: By analyzing a large volume of real dialogues, this module extracts and trains suitable response signals for various conversation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-714: Using multi-label classification, the system selects the optimal response for each dialogue context, significantly reducing user waiting time and enhancing conversation flow.
elements.sent-2411-715: The interruption detection module flexibly responds to user interruptions.
elements.sent-2411-716: Unlike traditional rule-based detection methods, this system builds an end-to-end detection model with multimodal input (audio and text) that not only identifies genuine user interruptions but also avoids misinterpreting background noise or unintended voice signals as interruptions.
elements.sent-2411-717: This scheme uses semantic analysis to determine whether the user truly intends to interrupt the system and can handle disjointed expressions when users mention named entities.
elements.sent-2411-718: The core of this system is a full-duplex interaction finite-state machine (FSM), which retrieves text snippets from ASR results every 300 milliseconds to decide whether to interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-719: Through continuous semantic analysis of user speech, the interruption model identifies meaningful user interruptions and avoids frequent interruptions caused by brief, meaningless responses (like "uh-huh").
elements.sent-2411-720: The model employs a pre-trained BERT-based text classifier and utilizes streaming input, ensuring that the system can process and analyze user speech in real-time as it is received.
elements.sent-2411-721: Additionally, the system includes a Discontinuous Expression module to handle user pauses when mentioning named entities.

elements.sent-2411-723: The advent of Large Language Models has significantly advanced generative AI development.

elements.sent-2411-733: VITA is an open-source multimodal large language model which aimed at enhancing multimodal interaction experiences.
elements.sent-2411-734: VITA can process multiple modalities, such as video, image, text, and audio, and achieves fluid human-computer interaction through a new duplex architecture involving two simultaneously operating models: one for generating responses to user queries, and another for continuously monitoring environmental inputs.
elements.sent-2411-735: When a new user query is detected, the generation model pauses, and the monitoring model processes the new query and generates an updated response.
elements.sent-2411-736: This setup enables VITA to support audio interruption, allowing users to ask new questions during system generation, with the system immediately pausing the current response to handle new input.
elements.sent-2411-737: VITA’s perception abilities are achieved through multimodal alignment and instruction fine-tuning, enabling it to switch automatically between different inputs.
elements.sent-2411-738: Additionally, VITA employs state tokens to distinguish user input types, such as query audio, background noise, and text input, facilitating wake-free interaction.
elements.sent-2411-739: VITA's enhanced listening module prevents unnecessary user feedback from interrupting system responses, improving robustness.
elements.sent-2411-740: Designed to facilitate seamless turn-taking and interruption handling, the model consists of several interconnected modules working in a coordinated sequence to optimize user experience.
elements.sent-2411-741: Starting with user input, the system uses a Voice Activity Detection (VAD) module to continuously monitor for incoming audio signals.
elements.sent-2411-742: As soon as a user starts speaking, VAD captures the input and immediately initiates processing by sending the audio data to the Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) module.
elements.sent-2411-743: This quick detection and response setup allows the system to react to user input without delay.
elements.sent-2411-744: Once ASR transcribes the audio into text, the transcription is passed to the Large Language Model (LLM), which generates a relevant response based on the user’s query.
elements.sent-2411-745: Meanwhile, the model is designed to be interruption-aware.
elements.sent-2411-746: During response generation, if VAD detects a new user input (indicating an interruption or a follow-up query), the system can promptly adjust its processing flow.
elements.sent-2411-747: In this case, the LLM temporarily pauses its current task, allowing ASR to transcribe the new input, which the LLM then uses to generate an updated response.
elements.sent-2411-748: This interruption capability is achieved through the model’s layered processing design, allowing for adaptive turn-taking that feels natural and responsive.
elements.sent-2411-749: The Text-to-Speech (TTS) module then converts the generated text response into audio, which is transmitted to the user via WebSocket.
elements.sent-2411-750: To further support interruption handling, TTS breaks down lengthy responses into smaller audio segments that are sent progressively.
elements.sent-2411-751: This segmentation allows the system to stop audio output instantly if an interruption occurs, switching to the new input without delay.
elements.sent-2411-752: Each segment is prepared and sent only after a brief VAD check, ensuring that the system is ready to pause and handle new input at any time.
elements.sent-2411-753: This interconnected processing chain—VAD detecting input, ASR transcribing, LLM generating responses, and TTS outputting segmented audio—creates a duplex interaction framework that balances response generation and user-driven interruptions.
elements.sent-2411-754: By seamlessly coordinating these components, the model provides a fluid, real-time dialogue experience that adapts to user interactions dynamically.. End-to-End Systems.

elements.sent-2411-756: Next, we introduce several representative works on interaction in end-to-end systems.. \quad$\bullet$ dGSLM.
elements.sent-2411-757: In end-to-end systems, the introduction of the dGSLM model marks a significant milestone in full-duplex technology development.
elements.sent-2411-758: Within the dGSLM framework, duplex technology is effectively implemented.
elements.sent-2411-759: This model demonstrates how to capture complex interactions within dialogues directly from raw audio data through generative spoken dialogue modeling, without relying on text.
elements.sent-2411-760: The core innovation of dGSLM is the dual-tower Transformer architecture, called the Dialogue Transformer Language Model (DLM), which uses a cross-attention mechanism to enable the system to process two parallel audio channels simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-761: Through this architecture, the model not only independently generates speech for each channel but also shares information between channels using cross-attention, effectively modeling silences and interaction events.
elements.sent-2411-762: It leverages the HuBERT encoder and HiFi-GAN decoder, combined with the dual-tower DLM, and is trained on 2,000 hours of dual-channel telephone conversation audio (Fisher dataset), where each speaker in a conversation is allocated an independent audio track.
elements.sent-2411-763: The dGSLM model transforms the audio on both channels into discrete tokens using HuBERT, and the DLM model autoregressively predicts the next audio token and its duration.
elements.sent-2411-764: Finally, the HiFi-GAN\cite{kong2020hifi decoder reconstructs the audio for both channels.}
elements.sent-2411-765: This approach differs significantly from traditional text-dependent spoken dialogue models, with a particular emphasis on modeling turn-taking and backchanneling capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-766: This capability gives dGSLM a notable advantage in duplex voice interaction, better mimicking the natural dynamics of human conversation.
elements.sent-2411-767: Through its duplex model design, dGSLM represents an essential step forward in interactive capabilities and provides a foundation for further advancements.. \quad$\bullet$ Moshi.
elements.sent-2411-768: As a novel full-duplex architecture, Moshi incorporates a rich array of design concepts.
elements.sent-2411-769: Unlike dGSLM, Moshi does not abandon the language model’s ability in text dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-770: Moshi’s architecture is based on the Helium language model and Mimi neural audio codec, both trained from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-771: Helium, as a large pre-trained text language model, provides strong reasoning capabilities, while Mimi handles audio signal encoding and decoding.
elements.sent-2411-772: To achieve real-time interaction, Moshi is designed as a multi-stream architecture, simultaneously processing "user" and "moshi" audio streams without explicitly modeling speaker turns.
elements.sent-2411-773: Moshi also introduces the "Inner Monologue" method within the "moshi" audio stream, a process that jointly models text and audio tokens during training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-774: This approach allows the model to fully utilize textual knowledge while maintaining speech-to-speech system characteristics, significantly enhancing generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-775: Mimi, a neural audio codec integrating semantic and acoustic information through residual vector quantization and knowledge distillation, captures high-quality user input audio and Moshi’s output voice efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-776: To jointly model Moshi and user audio streams alongside Moshi’s text tokens, Depth Transformer with streaming inference capabilities is employed.
elements.sent-2411-777: The Mimi encoder and decoder combine convolutional and Transformer layers, with causal convolutions, allowing for streaming operation.
elements.sent-2411-778: Moshi is pre-trained on unsupervised audio data to handle speech scenarios and then fine-tuned on the Fisher dataset to address overlapping speech and interruptions.
elements.sent-2411-779: Finally, the system is further optimized on a custom instruction-tuning dataset, ensuring robust performance across various interactive scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-780: Experimental results show that Moshi excels in speech modeling and spoken QA tasks, especially in latency, achieving a theoretical latency of 160 milliseconds and 200 milliseconds in practice, significantly lower than the typical 230 milliseconds in natural conversation, enhancing real-time interaction and conversation flow.. \quad$\bullet$ Parrot.
elements.sent-2411-781: Parrot~\cite{meng2024sd model incorporates multiple features specifically designed to enhance interaction in spoken dialogue.}
elements.sent-2411-782: It uses a dual-channel audio setup, where each channel represents a different speaker.
elements.sent-2411-783: This configuration allows Parrot to manage both sides of a conversation independently, facilitating real-time turn-taking.
elements.sent-2411-784: By distinguishing between the user’s input and the system’s response on separate channels, the model can listen and respond in parallel, creating a more natural conversational flow.
elements.sent-2411-785: To handle simultaneous speaker inputs effectively, Parrot employs a "next-token-pair prediction" mechanism, allowing it to predict tokens for both channels in a coordinated sequence.
elements.sent-2411-786: This approach helps the model manage conversational dynamics such as overlapping speech and smooth transitions between turns, adjusting response timing based on the user’s input.
elements.sent-2411-787: During inference, Parrot supports streaming input, enabling continuous processing of user audio on one channel while generating responses on the other.
elements.sent-2411-788: This streaming capability allows the model to respond to live spoken input in real-time, handling turn-taking, pauses, and interruptions dynamically.
elements.sent-2411-789: Unlike cascaded systems that rely on intermediate text conversions, Parrot processes audio directly, reducing latency and allowing immediate responses to spoken input.
elements.sent-2411-790: These interaction-focused design choices make Parrot highly responsive, enabling it to manage turn-taking naturally, respond to interruptions, and handle overlapping speech, \quad$\bullet$ Mini-Omni2.

elements.sent-2411-795: To achieve full-duplex interaction, Mini-Omni2 introduces an interrupt mechanism based on a limited instruction approach, trained on a specially constructed dataset with specific irq (interrupt) and n-irq (non-interrupt) state markers for model optimization.
elements.sent-2411-796: For training Mini-Omni2’s interruption functionality, the researchers used noisy speech data synthesized with specific command phrases (such as "Stop Omni") in various voices and tones to simulate scenarios where users might issue interrupt commands.
elements.sent-2411-797: The dataset also includes background noises, such as environmental sounds, music, and other dialogues, enhancing the model’s robustness in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-798: During training, Mini-Omni2 controls output flow through irq and n-irq state markers, generating these markers in real-time to determine whether to continue output.
elements.sent-2411-799: In this way, the model can immediately halt generation based on user instructions and switch to "listening" mode in real-time dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-800: The training data consists of long audio streams from which the model extracts and encodes user commands like "Stop Omni." Researchers inserted interrupt commands at various time points, marking data after the insertion point as irq (interrupt) and data before as n-irq (non-interrupt).
elements.sent-2411-801: This labeling method ensures that the model learns to accurately identify interrupt commands in complex audio inputs and respond appropriately.. \quad$\bullet$ SyncLLM.
elements.sent-2411-802: SyncLLM achieves full-duplex dialogue and interruption capabilities through multi-stream interleaving and chunk processing.
elements.sent-2411-803: SyncLLM divides the conversation's audio stream into fixed-sized chunks, each corresponding to a specific time interval.
elements.sent-2411-804: The model alternates between generating user and system speech segments within each time step (chunk), ensuring real-time system responses while processing user speech input.
elements.sent-2411-805: To maintain temporal synchronization with the user, SyncLLM predicts the user’s speech at each time step before generating each system chunk, using it as context to infer the system’s next response.
elements.sent-2411-806: This mechanism enables the system to keep pace with the conversation even with network latency.
elements.sent-2411-807: The chunk method allows SyncLLM to handle both user and system audio streams simultaneously, supporting complex dialogue features like speech overlap, interruption, and real-time feedback.
elements.sent-2411-808: Additionally, by using de-duplicated speech token sequences and periodic synchronization markers, the model efficiently performs chunk-level real-time inference, making conversation more fluid and natural.. \quad$\bullet$ OmniFlatten.
elements.sent-2411-809: Similar to SyncLLM, the OmniFlatten model achieves full-duplex and interruption functionality primarily through multi-stream data processing and progressive training.
elements.sent-2411-810: To enable full-duplex dialogue, the model adopts a multi-stream architecture that interleaves the user’s speech stream with the assistant’s speech and text streams into a single sequence for training, simplifying multimodal modeling and enhancing real-time capability.
elements.sent-2411-811: The model first aligns the text language model with modality through multitask supervised fine-tuning, enabling it to understand and generate both speech and text, ensuring basic capability for handling speech and text simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-812: Through a progressive training process, OmniFlatten attains full-duplex capability in three stages: initial training for half-duplex dialogue, then removing the user’s text stream to support real-time prediction with multi-stream data, and finally removing the assistant’s text stream to enable pure speech stream generation.
elements.sent-2411-813: These steps reduce reliance on text and decrease latency, allowing the system to generate voice responses while receiving user speech input.
elements.sent-2411-814: By using a block-by-block generation strategy, OmniFlatten divides the input and output speech sequences into fixed-size blocks, processing each segment in turn.
elements.sent-2411-815: This effectively implements streaming processing, ensuring low latency and high responsiveness in full-duplex dialogue, thereby providing a more natural response to user interruptions.. \quad$\bullet$ Freeze-Omni.
elements.sent-2411-816: To support duplex dialogue, Freeze-Omni~\cite{xiong2024freeze uses a chunk-level state prediction mechanism for natural turn-taking.}
elements.sent-2411-817: When the user begins speaking, a voice activity detection module identifies the audio input, prompting the model to process the audio chunk by chunk.
elements.sent-2411-818: After processing each chunk, the model's classification layer predicts the conversation state to determine the next action.
elements.sent-2411-819: There are three possible states: State 0, where the model continues listening for more input, assuming the user hasn’t completed their turn; State 1, where the model interrupts to provide an immediate response if a quick acknowledgment or feedback is needed; and State 2, where the model has completed processing the current user input and is ready to generate and output a response, thus transitioning smoothly into the response phase without further listening.
elements.sent-2411-820: This chunk-wise state prediction enables the model to decide effectively when to respond and when to continue listening, enhancing its ability to handle natural conversational cues and support interactive dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-821: Significant progress has been made in dialogues models, particularly in real-time interaction and semantic understanding, with notable achievements in streaming processing and full-duplex interaction.
elements.sent-2411-822: Current systems exhibit strong technical capabilities in reducing response latency, enhancing interruption handling, and improving the naturalness of conversation.
elements.sent-2411-823: However, existing spoken dialogues models still lack a unified system that can handle all forms of interaction seamlessly.
elements.sent-2411-824: Future research could explore new frameworks to better manage both user interruptions and the system’s ability to interrupt users, making interactions more natural.
elements.sent-2411-825: Additionally, standardized benchmarks for evaluating interaction capabilities remain underdeveloped.
elements.sent-2411-826: A unified evaluation benchmark would provide a consistent method for assessing and comparing the performance of different models, thereby advancing the development of more intelligent and responsive interaction systems.
elements.sent-2411-827: Training a spoken dialogue system is a complex, multi-stage process, with each stage relying on specific datasets to achieve distinct training objectives and enhance system performance.
elements.sent-2411-828: This section provides an in-depth analysis of the training resources about the spoken dialogue models, showcasing the data collection and processing methods at each stage and illustrating how these elements contribute to the system's intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-829: It further reveals how key steps, from foundational architecture to fine-tuning, shape the intelligent development of dialogue systems.
elements.sent-2411-830: To address the limitations of existing training spoken dialogue data and leverage the knowledge and reasoning capabilities of mature text-based models, many approaches involve Continue Training on pre-trained text language models.
elements.sent-2411-831: This training paradigm encompasses nearly all data types required to build a spoken dialogue system.
elements.sent-2411-832: The following sections focus on analyzing data acquisition and processing methods under this training flow, covering the following core stages: Text Language Model Pre-training, Post-Train for Audio Modal Adaption, Post-Train for Dual-Stream Audio Processing, Enhancing Conversational Abilities and Instruction Tuning.
elements.sent-2411-833: We have listed commonly used datasets for training in Table \ref{traindataset .}
elements.sent-2411-834: However, current spoken dialogue models lack exploration in music and sound.
elements.sent-2411-835: To support future development in spoken dialogue systems, we provide a list of common music and sound datasets in the appendix \ref{music datasets as a reference.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-025: Training resources about Text LLM Pre-training
elements.sent-2411-836: Text Language Model pre-training serves as the foundational stage for spoken dialogue models.
elements.sent-2411-837: Through unsupervised learning on large-scale text data, the model acquires knowledge of vocabulary, grammar, and contextual relationships, gaining essential knowledge and reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-838: Most spoken dialogue systems are built upon pre-existing open-source text language models (such as Llama~\cite{touvron2023llama , Palm~\cite{anil2023palm , etc).}}
elements.sent-2411-839: Although we does not delve into this stage in detail, it provides a solid foundation for the model’s natural language understanding and generation capabilities.

elements.sent-2411-841: In this stage, we introduce the audio modality, enabling the text language model to understand and generate speech while minimizing any potential loss of textual knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-842: This process is known as modal adaption or modal alignment.
elements.sent-2411-843: This multimodal structure incorporates an audio encoder with a codebook, helping the model recognize linguistic, emotional, and tonal information in speech.

elements.sent-2411-845: The primary goal at this stage is to align information from different modalities into a unified space or representation, allowing the model to correlate and comprehend such information.

elements.sent-2411-854: To ensure that the model possesses the ability to “listen while speaking”.
elements.sent-2411-855: Most research such as Moshi~\cite{defossez2024moshi and OmniFlatten~\cite{zhang2024omniflatten has implemented a dual audio-stream model: one audio stream generates model output, while the other captures user audio.}}
elements.sent-2411-856: The objective of this training phase is to enable the model’s dual-stream processing without requiring complex human-computer interaction modeling.
elements.sent-2411-857: Consequently, text dialogue data can be converted to speech and processed into dual-track audio format.
elements.sent-2411-858: However, text dialogue data typically contains content unsuitable for TTS conversion to speech (such as code, formulas, URLs) or long, formal dialogue passages that do not align with spoken language, as real dialogue is often more concise.
elements.sent-2411-859: Therefore, when synthesizing from text dialogue data, it is necessary to preprocess the text data.
elements.sent-2411-860: High-quality, open-source text dialogue data is first collected, including datasets like Alpaca~\cite{maeng2017alpaca , Moss, BelleCN, ultraChat~\cite{ding2023enhancing , and Open-Orca~\cite{OpenOrca .}}}
elements.sent-2411-861: To ensure suitability for speech synthesis (TTS), heuristic rules are applied to filter out samples with high proportions of non-text elements (such as code, mathematical expressions), samples exceeding 200 words, and samples containing rare symbols.
elements.sent-2411-862: After filtering the text, TTS models~\cite{du2024cosyvoice are used to synthesize speech for each turn in the dialogues.}
elements.sent-2411-863: For consistent voice effects, the model audio stream maintains a uniform voice, while the user audio stream is sampled with varied voices to enhance the model's robustness.
elements.sent-2411-864: The synthesized dialogue audio is arranged using simulation strategies to achieve natural timing, such as turn-taking, well-timed interruptions, and pauses to maintain fluency and naturalness.
elements.sent-2411-865: The final dialogue audio is organized in dual-channel format: the conversation begins with a user utterance, followed by alternating user and assistant turns.
elements.sent-2411-866: After each user turn, the assistant responds immediately; upon completion of the assistant’s turn, a sampled pause length is introduced to simulate the natural rhythm of alternating dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-867: To better simulate real scenarios, further data augmentation can be applied.
elements.sent-2411-868: For example, random gain adjustments can be applied to the user audio stream, and background noise randomly selected from datasets like MUSAN~\cite{snyder2015musan and DNS~\cite{reddy2001interspeech can be added to the user audio channel (OmniFlatten).}}
elements.sent-2411-869: To simulate echo effects from a user’s microphone, portions of the audio stream can be scaled down and added to the user’s audio stream with random delays between 100 to 500 milliseconds, along with reverberation-like enhancements, helping the model adapt to real-world environments.

elements.sent-2411-870: While the foundational model has been established, there remains a gap between this and a complete dialogue system.
elements.sent-2411-871: The above model utilizes non-overlapping dialogue audio, where one party remains silent while the other speaks, failing to fully simulate real conversational dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-872: Some speech datasets, such as Generative Expressive Conversational Speech Synthesis~\cite{liu2024generative and Fisher, contain dialogues from real-world settings, providing a basis for modeling interruptions and backchannels scenarios in voice dialogue systems.}
elements.sent-2411-873: Currently, there is no suitable dataset for real-world speech instructions.
elements.sent-2411-874: Most approaches use synthetic methods based on text instruction data to perform instruction tuning in this stage.
elements.sent-2411-875: Common text instruction datasets include Open Hermes and moss-002-sft-data, though they face similar challenges as text dialogue data, such as unsuitability for TTS conversion and inconsistency with spoken language conventions.
elements.sent-2411-876: Following the synthetic processes provided by Moshi and Llama-Omni, this aims to generate instruction data in the format of (SpeechInstruction, TextInstruction, TextResponse, SpeechResponse).
elements.sent-2411-877: The first method is synthetic generation from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-878: Contexts and summaries are first generated by sourcing high-quality text data from sources like Wikipedia and StackExchange, producing thematic paragraphs as the dialogue foundation, referred to as “context.” Based on these contexts, dialogue summaries are generated.
elements.sent-2411-879: Next, a specific prompt template guides the generation of complete dialogues, including context and requesting dialogues around the theme with roles as user and system.
elements.sent-2411-880: The model is prompted to exhibit knowledge on the topic and include interruptions (backchannels) and brief turn-taking, simulating the natural flow of conversation.
elements.sent-2411-881: To enhance dialogue diversity, additional instructions involving speech emotion and role-playing can be generated, requesting dialogues in specific tones or styles.
elements.sent-2411-882: Furthermore, dialogues containing spelling errors or misinformation are synthesized to train the system in handling scenarios where user clarification or repetition is required.
elements.sent-2411-883: Single-turn interactions on basic mathematics, grammar, and factual questions are also generated to ensure the system can handle simple factual tasks.
elements.sent-2411-884: Finally, scenarios involving ethical or NSFW requests are created to train the system in declining to answer under such conditions.
elements.sent-2411-885: The second method involves filtering and refining existing text instruction datasets.
elements.sent-2411-886: Initially, open-source text language models paraphrase text instructions to match spoken language traits, adding fillers like “uh” and “um” to mimic natural speech tone, while converting numbers and symbols into spoken language to ensure the instructions are concise and conversational.
elements.sent-2411-887: Generated text responses are also optimized to meet TTS output requirements, removing lengthy expressions and complex grammatical structures to make content clear and concise for TTS output.
elements.sent-2411-888: After adjusting the instruction and response text, a TTS system converts the text to audio.
elements.sent-2411-889: Fair and comprehensive evaluation of spoken dialogue models presents a multifaceted challenge.
elements.sent-2411-890: On the one hand, the field of spoken dialogue still lacks publicly available test sets, comprehensive evaluation metrics, and established benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-891: On the other hand, assessing the performance of spoken dialogue systems requires consideration from multiple perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-892: Basic aspects include the quality of generated speech, robustness, dialogue naturalness and accuracy, as well as response speed and generation time.
elements.sent-2411-893: Beyond these, more advanced evaluations are needed to assess multi-turn dialogue capabilities (such as long-form speech editing), interaction abilities, and the system's proficiency in audio and music understanding and generation.
elements.sent-2411-894: Given these requirements, and in line with the comprehensive expectations for spoken dialogue systems outlined in Section \ref{section21 , we will evaluate these systems from two angles: common evaluations and advanced evaluations.}
elements.sent-2411-895: Specifically, we will assess eleven key factors: speech generation quality, text intelligence, speech intelligence, audio and music generation, audio and music understanding, multilingual capability, context learning, interaction capability, streaming latency, multimodal capability, and the safety of dialogue systems.
elements.sent-2411-896: Finally, we will list the current benchmarks and summarize the common conclusions derived from them.

elements.sent-2411-897: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:main (a), text intelligence refers to the fundamental understanding and generation capabilities of the spoken dialogue model.}
elements.sent-2411-898: When evaluating text intelligence, the focus is solely on the semantic content generated by the model, without considering other aspects such as timbre, emotion, or style.
elements.sent-2411-899: In practical evaluations of this kind, some spoken dialogue models output only text~\cite{shu2023llasm, tang2023salmonn, chu2023qwen, chu2024qwen2, xue2023chat , while others generate both text and speech~\cite{defossez2024moshi,xie2024mini,xie2024miniomni2opensourcegpt4ovision , or only speech~\cite{zhang2024omniflatten .}}}
elements.sent-2411-900: Regardless of the output format, we are concerned only with the generated text or the transcribed text from the speech when evaluation the text intelligence in the spoken dialogue models.
elements.sent-2411-901: There are typically two categories of metrics and benchmarks used to assess text intelligence, MT-Metrics and Acc-Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-902: The details are outlined as follows: \quad$\bullet$ ACC-Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-903: A common approach to evaluating text intelligence is to use benchmarks typically~\cite{talmor2018commonsenseqa, liang2022holistic, zellers2019hellaswag, clark2018think, sakaguchi2021winogrande, chen2021evaluating, zhong2023agieval, mishra2021cross, wang2022super, feng2022mmdialog employed for large language models, such as the classic MMLU~\cite{hendrycks2020measuring and GSM-8K \cite{cobbe2021training .}}}
elements.sent-2411-904: These benchmarks often include complex multiple-choice questions, which assess the model's reasoning abilities through Acc-Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-905: Acc-Metrics refers to metrics that measure recognition accuracy, such as accuracy, F-score, and Mean Average Precision (mAP).
elements.sent-2411-906: It is noteworthy that these benchmarks often evaluate the text-based intelligence of spoken dialogue models from various perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-907: For example, MMLU \cite{hendrycks2020measuring and GSM-8K \cite{cobbe2021training are more focused on LLM's core knowledge, Flan \cite{longpre2023flan, wei2021finetuned and Self-instruct \cite{wang2022self are more focused on LLM's instruction following capability, CoQA \cite{reddy2019coqa and OpenAssistant \cite{kopf2024openassistant are more focused on LLM's conversational capability.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-908: These benchmarks often contain questions and corresponding answers.
elements.sent-2411-909: Most of these questions are close-ended questions with short answers, so that they can have good generalization ability, any model that can generate text answers can be evaluated with these benchmarks and accuracy and F-Score can be easily adopted as the evaluation metrics.. \quad$\bullet$ MT-Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-910: With the development of the LLMs, LLMs can follow instructions to accomplish many complex problems, so the scope of the evaluation was further expanded to include open-ended questions.
elements.sent-2411-911: These open-ended questions often lack standard answers, therefore it's difficult to measure them by common ACC-Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-912: A common approach is to measure the grammatical similarity between generated and reference utterances using the metrics used to measure grammatical similarity in mechanical translation (e.g. BLEU \cite{papineni2002bleu , METEOR \cite{banerjee2005meteor , ROUGE \cite{lin2004rouge ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-913: We collectively refer to these evaluation metrics as MT-Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-914: However, these metrics have certain limitations since one meaning has many different ways to convey.
elements.sent-2411-915: So there are some metrics like BertScore \cite{zhang2019bertscore focus on evaluating the semantic similarity between two sentences.}
elements.sent-2411-916: And there are also been some methods utilizing LLM to judge the effectiveness of the responses which focusing on human preference \cite{zheng2023judging, liu2023g .}
elements.sent-2411-917: The results of these large model-based especially GPT4o-based ratings of evaluation metrics demonstrated a high degree of correlation with human.. Speech Quality.
elements.sent-2411-918: Speech quality is one of the fundamental aspects for evaluating the performance of spoken dialogue systems, as it is closely tied to the experience of users.
elements.sent-2411-919: There are two common dimensions for assessing speech quality: the clarity and naturalness (expressiveness and prosody) of the generated audio, and the robustness of the generated speech, such as the presence of missing or extra words.
elements.sent-2411-920: The former is typically evaluated by using subjective MOS (Mean Opinion Score) ratings, while the latter is commonly assessed by using WER (Word Error Rate) or CER (Character Error Rate) metrics.. Streaming Latency.
elements.sent-2411-921: In addition to evaluating the quality of text understanding and generated speech, the speed at which a spoken dialogue system generates speech responses is also crucial.
elements.sent-2411-922: This necessitates the ability to stream both the comprehension and generation of speech in real time, achieving an effect of generating speech while speaking~\cite{zhang2024intrinsicvoice,defossez2024moshi,fang2024llama .}
elements.sent-2411-923: To assess the streaming performance of a model, one typically measures the time taken to generate the first token of speech (i.e., the waiting time after the user finishes speaking) and calculates the overall Real-Time Factor (RTF) of the spoken dialogue model's response.
elements.sent-2411-924: The RTF value is obtained by dividing the total duration of the speech segment generated by the model by the time taken by the model to generate that response.
elements.sent-2411-925: Evaluating the speech intelligence of spoken dialogue systems is one of the key aspects.
elements.sent-2411-926: The definition of speech intelligence in spoken dialogue systems is discussed in detail in Section \ref{section212 .}
elements.sent-2411-927: Given that speech intelligence encompasses a wide range of application scenarios, we address the evaluation separately for the understanding and generation components during the assessment.. \quad$\bullet$ Understanding.
elements.sent-2411-928: Ordinary cascaded spoken dialog models based on ASR getting text input will loss many paralinguistic information like speaking style, accent, emotion, etc. Thus many spoken dialogue models \cite{xue2023chat, lin2024paralinguistics, lin2024advancing devoted into helping dialog models understand the paralinguistic information.}
elements.sent-2411-929: Evaluating this capability can start from two aspects: a) the accuracy of the paralinguistic information's understanding, b) the ability of automatically generating appropriate and coherent content responses and acoustic information based on the varying acoustic input.
elements.sent-2411-930: For the former, since the classes of the paralinguistic information are always limited, for example, sentiments are generally categorized as neural, negative, positive.
elements.sent-2411-931: So the researchers always use Accuracy or F-Score to evaluate the models' paralinguistic information understanding capability.
elements.sent-2411-932: Recently, there are many studies \cite{goel2024audio, busso2008iemocap, poria2018meld, xue2023chat, lin2024advancing, firdaus2020meisd, busso2016msp available for researchers to use in identifying speech emotions in the dialogue scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-933: In addition to recognizing speech emotions, recent benchmarks \cite{ao2024sd, yang2024air has also begun to investigate the influence of speaker age, accent, and other factors on the evaluation of spoken dialogue models.}
elements.sent-2411-934: For the latter, recent work~\cite{xue2023chat has increasingly focused on the possibility of generating appropriate content responses based on acoustic information from the input.}
elements.sent-2411-935: The current evaluation methods usually transcript the output audio into text through Automatic Speech Recognition and then evaluate the relevance between generated content and the reference content in the internal dataset.
elements.sent-2411-936: Evaluations are usually conducted in text, so commonly used evaluation metrics are as the same as in the section \ref{eval_text , like BLEU and METEOR, which are used to measure the similarity between two sentences.}
elements.sent-2411-937: Currently, there is limited research exploring whether spoken dialogue models can autonomously generate appropriate acoustic responses based on varying acoustic information, making it a promising area for future investigation.. \quad$\bullet$ Generation.
elements.sent-2411-938: In the generation component, evaluating the speech intelligence of spoken dialogue systems primarily focuses on controllability, i.e., the ability of the dialogue model to respond in a user-specified style and timbre in the zero-shot scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-939: There are various dimensions to assess style, such as pitch, speech rate, energy, emotion, and accent, among others.

elements.sent-2411-942: Currently, there are few models that explore the generation of speech intelligence in spoken dialogue systems, and this area warrants further refinement and exploration in future work.

elements.sent-2411-948: For tasks like audio classification and SED, which involve fixed outputs, evaluation is relatively straightforward, typically using objective metrics such as accuracy or Mean Average Precision (mAP).

elements.sent-2411-951: In the case of audio-motivated creative writing, where the objective is to generate inventive descriptions from a given audio input, evaluation typically relies on subjective measures, given the divergent nature of the creative process involved.. \quad$\bullet$ Audio Generation.
elements.sent-2411-952: Additionally, on the audio generation side, producing high-quality audio should be considered an advanced capability for a conversational spoken dialogue model.
elements.sent-2411-953: However, as most current spoken dialogue systems lack the ability to generate audio, this remains an area for further exploration in the future end-to-end spoken dialogue systems.
elements.sent-2411-954: The evaluation of generated audio can draw from methods used in the text-to-audio domain~\cite{huang2023make2,huang2023make .}
elements.sent-2411-955: Typically, such evaluations focus on the quality of the generated audio itself, using metrics such as Mean Opinion Score (MOS) and the similarity between generated and target audio.
elements.sent-2411-956: Objective evaluation metrics for audio similarity often include Fréchet Distance (FD), Inception Score (IS), Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, Fréchet Audio Distance (FAD), and CLAP score.
elements.sent-2411-957: Specifically, Fréchet Audio Distance (FAD) \cite{kilgour2018fr is adapted from the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) to the audio domain and serves as a reference-free perceptual metric that quantifies the distance between the generated and ground-truth audio distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-958: The Inception Score (IS) is an effective metric that evaluates both the quality and diversity of generated audio.
elements.sent-2411-959: KL divergence is computed at the paired sample level between generated and ground-truth audio, based on the label distribution and averaged to produce a final result.
elements.sent-2411-960: Fréchet Distance (FD) evaluates the similarity between the generated and ground-truth audio distributions.
elements.sent-2411-961: FD, KL, and IS are built upon the PANNs model \cite{kong2020panns , which takes mel-spectrograms as input.}
elements.sent-2411-962: In contrast, FAD uses VGGish \cite{hershey2017cnn as an audio classifier, processing raw audio waveforms as input.}
elements.sent-2411-963: The CLAP score, adapted from the CLIP score \cite{hessel2021clipscore , is a reference-free metric used to assess audio-text alignment and strongly correlates with human perception.}

elements.sent-2411-972: A naive idea is to directly evaluate spoken dialogue models' capability in speech-to-speech or speech-to-text translation tasks \cite{ jia2022cvss,wang2020covost1 .}

elements.sent-2411-976: In most cases, when a user asks a question in a different language or with a distinct accent, the model is expected to automatically respond in the same language that the user is using.
elements.sent-2411-977: In this context, it seems more reasonable to evaluate the accuracy of the model’s generated speech in terms of language identification, combined with subjective human assessments, as a more intuitive and appropriate evaluation method.. Context Learning.

elements.sent-2411-980: Typically, the evaluation of a spoken dialogue model's context learning ability depends on specific long-duration dialogue test sets, after which standard MT-Metrics or Acc-Metrics used in text intelligence evaluations can be applied.
elements.sent-2411-981: For instance, a model's context learning capability can be assessed by evaluating its QA performance based on the given context \cite{lipping2022clotho .}
elements.sent-2411-982: However, it is important to note the relevance of editing scenarios in long-duration spoken dialogues.

elements.sent-2411-984: So how to evaluate the model's online understanding ability is still needed further study.. Interaction Capability.
elements.sent-2411-985: Interactive ability is also an essential metric for assessing the advanced capabilities of spoken dialogue systems.

elements.sent-2411-987: In this context, it is crucial to evaluate whether the spoken dialogue model can promptly comprehend the user's new input and halt its current response.

elements.sent-2411-990: In addition, in real-world scenarios, beyond basic interruptions, various discourse markers such as "okay", "haha" are often used to indicate interaction.

elements.sent-2411-1004: We list the common benchmarks for evaluating voice dialogue systems in the table\ref{table:benchmark , and briefly introduce each benchmark in this section.. \quad$\bullet$ VoiceBench.}
elements.sent-2411-1005: VoiceBench's~\cite{chen2024voicebench Key evaluation dimensions include general knowledge, instruction-following ability, and safety compliance.}
elements.sent-2411-1006: The benchmark incorporates both synthetic and real spoken instructions to simulate diverse speaker styles, environmental conditions, and content variations.
elements.sent-2411-1007: It challenges systems with tasks involving accent adaptability, handling noisy environments, and robustness against content irregularities such as grammatical errors, disfluencies, and mispronunciations.
elements.sent-2411-1008: Additionally, it explores the systems' resilience under varying speaker characteristics (age, pitch, and speaking speed) and environmental challenges like reverberation, background noise, and far-field effects.
elements.sent-2411-1009: Tasks in content recognition cover phoneme recognition, automatic speech recognition, keyword spotting, and query-by-example spoken term detection, focusing on transcription and content detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-1010: Speaker modeling involves tasks like speaker identification, automatic speaker verification, and speaker diarization to assess speaker-related features.
elements.sent-2411-1011: Semantic understanding includes intent classification and slot filling, testing models' ability to infer high-level meaning directly from raw audio.
elements.sent-2411-1012: Paralinguistic analysis focuses on emotion recognition, capturing models' ability to interpret affective cues from speech.
elements.sent-2411-1013: The evaluation framework uses publicly available datasets and conventional metrics to provide a standardized testbed for assessing generalizability and task-specific performance.. \quad$\bullet$ AudioBench.
elements.sent-2411-1014: AudioBench~\cite{wang2024audiobench evaluates spoken dialogue models across three primary dimensions: speech understanding, audio scene understanding, and voice (paralinguistic) understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-1015: It encompasses eight distinct tasks and leverages 26 datasets, including seven newly developed datasets.
elements.sent-2411-1016: The evaluation emphasizes models' ability to handle instruction-following tasks conditioned on audio signals, addressing aspects such as speech recognition accuracy, environmental sound interpretation, and paralinguistic feature extraction (e.g., emotion, gender, accent).. \quad$\bullet$ AirBench.
elements.sent-2411-1017: AIR-Bench~\cite{yang2024air assesses the capabilities of Spoken dialogue models to understand and interact based on various audio types, including human speech, natural sounds, and music.}
elements.sent-2411-1018: It consists of two primary components: a foundation benchmark with 19 specific audio tasks and over 19,000 single-choice questions, and a chat benchmark featuring more than 2,000 open-ended audio-prompted questions.
elements.sent-2411-1019: The foundation benchmark evaluates fundamental skills such as speech recognition, acoustic scene classification, and music genre identification, focusing on specific subtasks to diagnose model weaknesses.
elements.sent-2411-1020: The chat benchmark tests the models' ability to handle complex, real-world audio-based queries, including mixed audio with varying loudness and temporal offsets.
elements.sent-2411-1021: AIR-Bench introduces a novel audio mixing strategy to simulate complex real-world scenarios and employs GPT-4-based evaluation to judge model-generated hypotheses against reference answers.. \quad$\bullet$ SpokenWOZ.
elements.sent-2411-1022: SpokenWOZ~\cite{si2024spokenwoz evaluates task-oriented dialogue (TOD) systems in spoken scenarios, addressing challenges unique to spoken conversations, such as incremental processing, disfluencies, incomplete utterances, and Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) noise.}
elements.sent-2411-1023: It introduces novel metrics to assess performance in tasks like cross-turn slot detection and reasoning slot detection, which require integrating information across multiple turns and reasoning from implicit cues.
elements.sent-2411-1024: The benchmark encompasses multi-domain, human-to-human dialogues with diverse speech characteristics, testing systems on both textual and auditory inputs through large-scale annotated datasets with over 200,000 utterances and 249 hours of audio \quad$\bullet$ SD-EVAL.
elements.sent-2411-1025: SD-Eval~\cite{ao2024sd evaluates spoken dialogue models across multiple dimensions, focusing on both spoken understanding and response generation beyond textual content.}
elements.sent-2411-1026: It assesses models' abilities to process three key types of information embedded in speech: content (e.g., linguistic meaning), paralinguistic cues (e.g., emotion, accent, age), and environmental context (e.g., background sounds).
elements.sent-2411-1027: The benchmark consists of four sub-tasks—emotion, accent, age, and environment—constructed from diverse datasets and totaling 7,303 utterances spanning 8.76 hours.. \quad$\bullet$ SuperCLUE.
elements.sent-2411-1028: SuperCLUE evaluates spoken dialogue systems across four main dimensions: voice interaction, general capabilities, scenario applications, and response speed.
elements.sent-2411-1029: Key metrics include interruption recognition, speech tone adjustment, semantic understanding, naturalness of speech, and memory accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-1030: Additionally, it measures real-time data retrieval, reasoning ability, compliance with commands, and multilingual translation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-1031: Scenario-specific applications like emotional counseling, health consultations, and customer service are assessed for precision and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-1032: The final aspect is response timeliness, focusing on latency and delay management.However, this benchmark is not open source and focuse on Mandarine ability \quad$\bullet$ MMAU.
elements.sent-2411-1033: MMAU~\cite{sakshi2024mmau evaluates spoken dialogue models across multiple dimensions, encompassing 27 distinct tasks divided into reasoning and information extraction categories.}
elements.sent-2411-1034: It assesses models on their ability to comprehend and reason about speech, sound, and music by leveraging advanced cognitive skills and domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-1035: Key evaluated areas include temporal event reasoning, speaker role mapping, emotional tone interpretation, eco-acoustic knowledge, phonemic stress pattern analysis, and melodic structure interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-1036: It examines not just basic recognition or transcription capabilities but also models' proficiency in complex reasoning, contextual understanding, and the ability to extract and apply world knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-1037: Additionally, MMAU scrutinizes performance consistency across varying difficulty levels, testing systems' depth of reasoning and robustness in real-world audio scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1038: In this work, we systematically review the research related to spoken dialogue models, categorizing it according to two paradigms: cascaded spoken dialogue models and end-to-end spoken dialogue models.
elements.sent-2411-1039: Additionally, we provide a detailed overview of the core technologies behind spoken dialogue models, including speech representation, training paradigms, streaming duplex systems, and interaction mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-1040: In the speech representation module, we classify and explain the representations from both the input and output perspectives, focusing on different types of semantic and acoustic representations.
elements.sent-2411-1041: In the training paradigm module, we thoroughly discuss five modalities of alignment for spoken dialogue models, multi-stage training strategies, model architectures, and generation paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-1042: Following this, we provide an in-depth analysis of streaming input and output for spoken dialogue models, as well as the related duplex interaction technologies.
elements.sent-2411-1043: Finally, we compile key training resources, evaluation metrics, and benchmarks relevant to spoken dialogue models.
elements.sent-2411-1044: We specifically address the evaluation of different levels of intelligence in spoken dialogue models across various scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1045: It is important to note that, given that spoken dialogue models are a relatively new and emerging technology, many aspects such as semantic and acoustic representations, still lack well-established paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-1046: Therefore, at the end of each section, we include a dedicated discussion module to explore these open issues.
elements.sent-2411-1047: We hope that this survey will contribute to the further development of the field of spoken dialogue systems.. \bibliographystyle{plain .}
